confidence.disclosure: Reconstructed principally from Gulbadan Begum's Humayun-nama (written by his sister, an eyewitness to much of his life), Jauhar Aftabchi's Tazkirat al-Waqiat (written by Humayun's personal attendant), and Abul Fazl's Akbar-nama. The fifteen-year exile (1540-1555) is unusually well documented because both Gulbadan and Jauhar were with him for much of it. Some intermediate stops in Sind and the initial Persian journey are dated approximately.

sources: Ishwari Prasad, The Life and Times of Humayun (1955)

journey.event: Born in Kabul on 6 March 1508 to Babur and Mahim Begum.
Commanded the right wing of Babur's army at the First Battle of Panipat that founded the Mughal empire.
Fought at the Battle of Khanwa against Rana Sanga.
Enthroned at Agra on 29 December 1530 after the death of Babur.
Captured the Malwa hilltop capital of Mandu from Bahadur Shah of Gujarat.
Took the Gujarat fortress of Champaner, reputedly climbing the cliff on iron spikes.
Defeated by Sher Shah Suri at the Battle of Chausa on the Ganges on 26 June 1539; escaped by swimming the river.
Decisively defeated by Sher Shah Suri at the Battle of Kannauj on 17 May 1540, losing the empire and beginning fifteen years of exile.
Fled from Agra with a small retinue as Sher Shah's forces advanced.
Held a family council at Lahore but failed to unite his brothers for a counter-offensive.
Besieged Bhakkar in Sind for several months without success.
Attempted unsuccessfully to take Sehwan in Sind.
Given shelter by the Hindu Rana of Umarkot in Sind, where his son Akbar was born on 15 October 1542.
Quarrelled with his brother Mirza Hindal at Jun in Sind.
Reached Kandahar but his brother Askari refused him entry; narrowly escaped capture.
Crossed the Afghan-Persian border into the Sistan region.
Received as an honoured guest at Herat by the Safavid governor.
Visited the shrine of Imam Reza at Mashhad en route to meet Shah Tahmasp.
Travelled through Nishapur on the way west.
Continued westward through Sabzevar.
Passed through Damghan in Qumes.
Reached Rayy near modern Tehran.
Arrived at the Safavid capital Qazvin.
Formally received by Shah Tahmasp I near Sultaniyya; granted 12,000 troops to reconquer Kandahar.
Returned east through Mashhad with the Safavid auxiliary force.
Besieged and retook Kandahar with Safavid help in September 1545.
Captured Kabul from his brother Kamran in November 1545.
Lost Kabul to Kamran again, then retook it for the second time.
Defeated Kamran a third and final time and held Kabul permanently.
Captured and blinded Kamran, ending the fraternal civil war.
Crossed the Khyber and descended into Peshawar at the start of the reconquest.
Took Lahore in February 1555 against weak Suri resistance.
Defeated Sikandar Shah Suri at the decisive Battle of Sirhind in June 1555.
Re-entered Delhi in triumph on 23 July 1555 after fifteen years of exile.
Died in Delhi on 27 January 1556 after falling down the stairs of his library at the Purana Qila.
